content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

August 1, 1997

Laurence S. Zakson
Reich, Adell, Crost & Cvitan
501 Shatto Place, Suite 100
Los Angeles, CA 90020-1792

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-97-352

Dear Mr. Zakson:

This is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹ You have provided no specific facts or named an individual on whose
behalf you seek advice. Therefore, we render you informal assistance.²

QUESTION

How is the value of a room provided to a political organization determined for purposes
of reporting a non-monetary contribution?

CONCLUSION

The value of the campaign contribution made by providing a meeting space to a political
organization is the rate the donor would charge in the regular course of business to members of
the public.

FACTS

An organization has meeting space that it makes available to the public at a nominal
charge of usually less than $100 per day. The organization wishes to donate this same meeting
space to a political organization, other than its own sponsored committee.

_________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329.)

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. I-97-352
Page 2

APPLICABLE LAW

Government Code section 82015 defines a "contribution," in part, as the granting of
discounts or rebates not extended to the public generally.

Regulation 18215 provides that a contribution includes "[a]ny goods or services received
by or behested by a candidate or committee at no charge or at a discount from the fair market
value, unless the discount is given in the regular course of business to members of the public."

ANALYSIS

Here an organization provides meeting space to members of the public for a nominal
charge, which may reflect a discount from the fair market value. The organization wishes to
donate that same meeting space to a political organization.

Under the Act, values of non-monetary contributions must be reported at the fair market
value unless a discount is given in the regular course of business to members of the public.
According to your facts, the customary charge for the meeting space is a rate generally available
to members of the public. Therefore, the exception for discounts made available to the general
public will apply. The reportable value of the contribution to the political organization for use of
the meeting space will be the value of that rate the donor charges in the regular course of
business to members of the public. (Regulation 18215(b)(3).)

If you have any further questions, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,


Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Margaret E. Figersold
Political Reform Consultant
